**Ajwain**, also called **ajowan caraway**, **carom seeds**, **owa**, **vaamu**, **mamam**, and **asamodagam**, is a spice used often used in South Asian, Middle Eastern, and North/East African cooking. It is sometimes mistakenly called bishop's weed due to their similar appearance, and it may be confused with lovage seeds.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The plant is similar in appearance to parsley. Ajwain pods or seeds are the small seed-like fruits of the plant. They are egg-shaped and gray in color. The flavor of the seeds is sharp and reminiscent of cumin, oregano, thyme, and anise.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Ajwain is commonly used as a flavoring in South Asian cooking. It is typically either toasted or oil-fried before use, which helps mellow and develop the flavor.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) A little of this spice goes a long way.
